content: Hi, I'm Dakota, and I'm a Software QA Lead at a healthcare company. I'm feeling really frustrated with my current role and I'm not sure if this is the right career for me anymore.
Hi Dakota, sorry to hear that you're feeling frustrated. Can you tell me more about what's leading to these feelings? Is it the work itself, the company, or something else?

I think it's a combination of everything. I love working in tech, but the long hours and high-stress environment are taking a toll on me. I'm also feeling really undervalued and unappreciated in my current role.
It sounds like you're feeling overwhelmed and undervalued in your current role. Have you considered exploring other areas of tech, or possibly making a career change altogether?

Yeah, I've been thinking about that a lot lately. I've always been interested in speedcubing, and I've even started competing in some local tournaments. Maybe I could find a way to use my problem-solving skills to help others.

That's a great idea! Using your problem-solving skills to help others is a wonderful way to make a positive impact. What kind of tournaments have you been competing in, and what do you hope to achieve with your speedcubing?

I've been competing in some local Rubik's Cube tournaments, and I'm really enjoying it. I'd love to start competing at the national level and see where it takes me. I'm thinking of investing in a new cube and practicing regularly.

It sounds like you're really committed to pursuing your passion for speedcubing. Investing in a new cube and practicing regularly are great steps to take. Have you thought about how you'll balance your speedcubing with your current work as a Software QA Lead?

To be honest, I'm not sure. I'm thinking of scaling back my hours as a Software QA Lead and focusing more on my speedcubing. I've got a tournament coming up on May 20th, and I'm hoping it will be a good opportunity to get my name out there.

It's great that you have a tournament lined up! Scaling back your hours as a Software QA Lead could be a good way to free up time and energy to focus on your speedcubing. What's the plan for your training leading up to the tournament, and how are you preparing yourself mentally and physically?

I'm planning to practice regularly and focus on improving my solves. I'm also going to work on my mental game and try to stay focused under pressure. I'm hoping to beat my personal best time of 15.2 seconds and make it to the finals.

That sounds like a great plan! Improving your solves and working on your mental game will definitely help you achieve your goals. What do you think is the most challenging part of competing in speedcubing tournaments, and how do you overcome those challenges?
